sentence_reviews.text: External or constructed signals specify desired predictions, actions, rankings, or behavior.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: supervised learning, classification/regression, ranking losses, active learning, semi/weakly supervised learning, and reinforcement learning (lines 3-14, 515-519, and 588-606); AIMA contents.html: supervised learning, decision/reward material, and reinforcement learning from rewards (lines 495-508 and 599-615).
sentence_reviews.notes: The sources support labels/examples, losses, ranking, rewards, and preferences/decision signals as learning signals. The exact compact definition is atlas synthesis.

sentence_reviews.text: The role of Labels, targets, rewards & preferences is to connect Statistics & experimental design, Game theory & decision theory to downstream capabilities including Supervised learning, Semi-supervised & weak supervision, Reinforcement learning.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: statistics, decision theory, supervised learning, semi/weakly supervised learning, and reinforcement learning (lines 106-178 and 3-14, 588-606); AIMA contents.html: making decisions, game theory, supervised learning, and reinforcement learning (lines 435-480, 495-508, and 599-615).
sentence_reviews.notes: Each major component is supported across the inspected sources, but the connector wording is a synthesized atlas role statement.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: Chapter 1.2 'Supervised learning' and classification/regression sections (lines 3-8); AIMA contents.html: Chapter 19.2 'Supervised Learning' (lines 495-503).
sentence_reviews.notes: Supervised learning is directly supported as learning from labeled examples/tasks. 'Substrate for' is not source wording.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: Chapter 19.3 'Semi-supervised learning' and 19.7 'Weakly supervised learning' (lines 588-606).
sentence_reviews.notes: The relevant learning settings are directly named; the substrate relation is a reasonable synthesis.

sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: Chapter 1.4 'Reinforcement learning' (line 14); AIMA contents.html: Chapter 22.1 'Learning from Rewards' and reward shaping (lines 599-615).
sentence_reviews.notes: Rewards are directly tied to reinforcement learning in AIMA. The exact substrate wording is atlas synthesis.

sentence_reviews.text: Encoding choices can discard information, amplify bias, or make important invariances difficult to learn. Its direct dependencies include Statistics & experimental design, Game theory & decision theory.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: statistics, decision theory, caveats, and loss/model-selection sections (lines 22-24, 106-178); AIMA contents.html: fairness and bias, game theory, decision making (lines 745-749 and 435-480).
sentence_reviews.notes: Statistics and game/decision theory are supported, and bias is a relevant AI concern. The encoding-loss and invariance claims are broader than the inspected evidence.

sentence_reviews.text: Labels, targets, rewards & preferences rests on the formal or computational capabilities supplied by Statistics & experimental design.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: statistics chapter, empirical risk minimization, cross-validation, and supervised learning sections (lines 106-147 and 3-8).
sentence_reviews.notes: The evidence supports statistical estimation and evaluation around supervised signals. Experimental design is implicit.

sentence_reviews.text: Labels, targets, rewards & preferences rests on the formal or computational capabilities supplied by Game theory & decision theory.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: Chapter 5 'Decision Theory' and active learning decision-theoretic approach (lines 148-178 and 596-599); AIMA contents.html: game theory, sequential decisions, MDPs, and rewards (lines 435-480 and 599-615).
sentence_reviews.notes: Decision and reward material supports the dependency; game theory is more relevant to preferences and multiagent settings than to all supervision signals.

sentence_reviews.text: Labels, targets, rewards & preferences rests on the formal or computational capabilities supplied by Information theory.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: PML book1 long TOC: information theory chapter, entropy/cross entropy/KL/mutual information, and information-theoretic active learning (lines 179-205 and 596-599).
sentence_reviews.notes: Information-theoretic losses and active learning are relevant, but the evidence does not establish information theory as a general dependency for labels, rewards, and preferences.

edge_reviews.notes: Statistical estimation, empirical risk, validation, and supervised-learning examples make this required/core edge defensible.
Decision-theory and reward/preference contexts support a common dependency, but not all supervision signals require game theory.
Information theory is relevant to entropy, cross-entropy, mutual information, and active learning, but the source support is narrower than the rationale.
